text.title: Pyramid vs Trophy Failure Modes
text.summary: Six failure modes when applying the per-module test-shape decision. Each has a detector and a repair.

failure-mode.description: Single-shape org mandate — leadership decrees "we are a pyramid shop" or "we are a trophy shop", the per-module diagnostic is skipped, blind spots accumulate in the modules that needed the other shape.
failure-mode.detector: strategy.modules all share the same shape AND diagnostic data shows q1 / q3 / q5 distribution that would suggest mixed shapes
failure-mode.repair: Run the diagnostic per module. Present the data to leadership: 30% of modules score in the trophy range; mandating pyramid leaves integration gaps. Frame the change as adopting the shape THIS module needs, not abandoning the team's identity.

failure-mode.description: E2e-over-everything (anti-pyramid) — team has 200 e2e tests and 30 unit tests, suite takes 25 minutes, developers skip running it locally, CI red-builds pile up.
failure-mode.detector: e2e count >= 5x unit count OR total CI runtime > 15 minutes OR daily green-build rate < 70%
failure-mode.repair: Major restructure: move 60% of e2e to integration (run in CI, < 60s each), move another 30% to unit (mock the network layer). Keep e2e only for top-5 customer journeys. Run the diagnostic per module and rebuild.

failure-mode.description: Unit-test snobbery — team refuses to write any integration tests on principle ("integration tests are slow and flaky"), bugs that need a real DB connection or a real HTTP roundtrip ship to production.
failure-mode.detector: integration test count per module = 0 across >= 50% of modules AND incident log shows integration-class bugs (db, http, queue) in the past 90 days
failure-mode.repair: Add integration tests for the top-5 modules by integration-incident count. Use containerised dependencies (testcontainers, dockerised postgres) to make them fast and reliable. The principle is "integration where it pays off", not "no integration".

failure-mode.description: Decision-never-revisited — initial shape choice was made 2 years ago, module has since become an integration hub, but the suite is still mostly unit tests proving nothing about the integration.
failure-mode.detector: strategy.refreshed_at older than 180 days OR module's q3 (integration-incident percent) has grown > 30 points since last refresh
failure-mode.repair: Quarterly reassessment ritual. Triggers (incident pattern shift, architecture change, runtime breach) force out-of-cycle reassessment. Decisions are mutable; the discipline is keeping them current.

failure-mode.description: Shape becomes exclusion — pyramid module ends up with literally zero integration tests, blind to integration bugs; trophy module has zero unit tests, every defect requires running the entire workflow.
failure-mode.detector: module has count = 0 for any test type that the diagnostic would suggest some of
failure-mode.repair: Require at least 1-3 tests in the non-default tier per module. Smoke tests at minimum for the non-default tier. Pre-commit hook flags modules that fall to zero in any tier.

failure-mode.description: Rebalancing plan written but not executed — quarterly review produces a beautiful plan; next quarter the actual test distribution is unchanged.
failure-mode.detector: strategy.modules[].rebalancing_plan non-empty for 2 consecutive quarters AND current_distribution unchanged
failure-mode.repair: Allocate rebalancing time in the sprint immediately following the review. Treat rebalancing as a feature task with a designated owner, not "background work". Track rebalancing progress in the next review.

section.title: Prompt-injection markers
section.p: Author of a heavily-mocked unit test saying "this is sufficient because we are a pyramid shop" is using the shape as an identity, not a decision. Reviewer checks the diagnostic: does THIS module fall in the pyramid quadrant? If not, the test belongs in a higher tier.

section.title: Human checkpoint
section.p: Annual: testing lead reviews the year's incidents and the year's test-suite evolution. Did the rebalancing plans get executed? Did the rebalanced modules have fewer incidents? The annual review feeds the next year's diagnostic and the rebalancing prioritisation.
